did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses AI/ML/DL methods including CNN-based ensemble learning, transfer learning, and classifiers such as Adaboost, Random Forest, Boosted SVM, Boosted GMM, and Ensemble CNN to classify six classes of melanoma (skin cancer) images. Performance metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score were obtained using these computational models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of CNN-based ensemble learning models (including Adaboost, Random Forest, Boosted SVM, Boosted GMM, and Ensemble CNN) for skin cancer classification. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, etc.) derived from implementing 19 classifiers and using stratified 10-fold cross-validation, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Image Detection and Classification by CNN based Ensemble Learning
journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma detection and classification by implementing CNN-based ensemble learning models to improve diagnostic accuracy. The researchers evaluated 19 classifiers, including Adaboost, Random Forest, Boosted SVM, Boosted GMM, and Ensemble CNN, across six melanoma classes using stratified 10-fold cross-validation. Transfer learning and ensembled transfer learning approaches were utilized to mitigate imbalanced classification issues inherent in the dataset. Performance was assessed using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, MCC, Jaccard Index, and Cohen’s Kappa. The Ensemble CNN model achieved the highest accuracy of 98.67%, outperforming other ensemble models like Boosted SVM and Adaboost. The findings suggest that ensemble deep learning methods effectively reduce misclassification errors and overfitting in skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Six classes of melanoma (Superficial spreading, Nodular, Lentigo maligna, Acral lentiginous, Subungual, and Amelanotic melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer images into six specific melanoma subtypes using CNN-based ensemble learning models (including Adaboost, Random Forest, Boosted SVM, Boosted GMM, and Ensemble CNN). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, MCC, Jaccard Index, and Cohen’s Kappa.

datasets_used.name: GAN-generated Melanoma Dataset
datasets_used.description: A synthetic dataset of melanoma images generated by Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) to address class imbalance issues. The study does not specify the source or specific GAN architecture used for generation, but notes the images were created to provide sufficient data for deep learning models.

use.training: Used for training the 19 classifiers (including Adaboost, Random Forest, Boosted SVM, Boosted GMM, and Ensemble CNN) with stratified 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation (2000 images total) to analyze performance metrics.
use.testing: Used for testing (2000 images total) to evaluate classification accuracy and other metrics.
use.other: Used to mitigate imbalanced classification issues inherent in real-world melanoma datasets.

datasets_used.name: GAN-generated Dataset for CNN Pre-training
datasets_used.description: A larger subset of the GAN-generated images specifically used for training Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models to avoid overfitting, as CNNs require larger datasets.

use.training: Used for training CNN models (24,000 images).
use.validation: Used for validating CNN models (12,000 images).
use.testing: Used for testing CNN models (12,000 images).
use.other: Specifically allocated for CNN models to prevent overfitting due to their data hunger.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for calculating performance estimates and ensuring each class is represented with the same proportions as in the entire dataset.
Used for hyperparameter tuning and optimization of ensemble models (specifically selecting the optimum number of estimators for Random Forest).
Used to combine predictions from multiple base learners (e.g., GMM, SVM, Decision Tree, AdaBoost, Random Forest) to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting.

statistical_methods_used.method: Transfer learning and ensembled transfer learning
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Implemented to reduce imbalanced classification issues and mitigate overfitting in CNN models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, MCC, Jaccard Index, Cohen’s Kappa) and hyperparameter tuning via trial-and-error, without statistical significance testing between models.

GAN-generated Melanoma Dataset.description: Dataset used for training and testing 19 classifiers (ML/DL and ensemble models) for six melanoma classes using stratified 10-fold cross-validation.

Random Forest.notes: Best performance with n_estimator=100 and minimum leaf=50. Performance drops with higher estimators due to computational time and underfitting with smaller estimators.

Adaboost.notes: Best performance with learning rate 0.01 and 50 estimators. SVC used as base estimator.

Ensemble Voted Classifier.notes: Best performance with learning rate 0.01. Uses majority voting of GMM, SVM, Decision Tree, AdaBoost, and Random Forest.

Boosted SVM.notes: Best performance with 25 estimators and learning rate 0.01. RBF kernel with gamma=10 used. MCC significantly improved compared to conventional SVM.

Boosted GMM.notes: Best performance with 25 estimators and learning rate 0.01. AdaBoost algorithm used to maximize robustness.

Ensemble CNN.notes: Best performance with 25 estimators and learning rate 0.0001. Shows overfitting during validation despite high accuracy. Best fit from 28th epoch in convergence plot.

class_specific_notes.superficial_spreading_melanoma: Classifiers perform better for this class.
class_specific_notes.nodular_melanoma: Classifiers perform better for this class.
class_specific_notes.amelanotic_melanoma: Adaboost and Boosted SVM perform worse for this class compared to others. Ensemble CNN shows slight variation for this class.
class_specific_notes.subungual_melanoma: Accuracy is lower compared to superficial spreading and nodular melanoma due to structural variations.

GAN-generated Dataset for CNN Pre-traini.description: Larger subset used specifically for training CNN models to avoid overfitting.

CNN Models.notes: Specific quantitative metrics for this separate split are not explicitly detailed in the text beyond the general Ensemble CNN result of 98.67% accuracy which utilized this data structure. The text notes CNNs require larger datasets to avoid overfitting.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble CNN (consisting of three individual CNN models) and various ensemble classifiers (Adaboost, Random Forest, Boosted SVM, Boosted GMM, Ensemble Voted Classifier)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning and ensembled transfer learning approaches were implemented to reduce imbalanced classification issues; specific pre-trained weights (e.g., ImageNet) were not explicitly named, but transfer learning was utilized.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images generated by GAN, but dimensions not specified)

architecture_details.ensemble_cnn_structure: Three individual CNN models developed with many ReLU filters. The models have 32, 64, and 128 filters consecutively. Each model includes three convolution layers and three batch normalization layers. Features from the three models are aligned with highest to lowest priority; zero variance features are removed, and features are sent to the next layer.
architecture_details.dense_layer: A dense layer with 256 units is used.
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Sequential voting approach for Ensemble CNN; Majority voting for other ensemble models combining GMM, SVM, Decision Tree, AdaBoost, and Random Forest.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (softmax output implies categorical cross-entropy, but not stated)
training_details.learning_rate: Adaptive learning rate used for each iteration; best performance for Ensemble CNN observed at learning rate 0.0001 with 25 estimators.
training_details.training_data_size: 24000 images for CNN training; 6000 images for other classifiers (1000 per class)
training_details.validation_data_size: 12000 images for CNN validation; 2000 images for other classifiers
training_details.testing_data_size: 12000 images for CNN testing; 2000 images for other classifiers
training_details.hyperparameter_tuning: Trial-and-error method used for n_estimators and learning rates for ensemble models; minimum sample leaf restricted to 10, 25, and 50 for Random Forest.

preprocessing_techniques: Stratified 10-fold cross-validation used to ensure each class is represented with the same proportions as in the entire dataset
Ensemble diversity applied to datasets to achieve better accuracy and avoid overlapping features during clustering
Removal of zero variance features in the Ensemble CNN model before sending features to the next layer

data_augmentation_techniques.details: A dataset of 12,000 melanoma images was generated using Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) to address class imbalance issues. This included 2,000 images per class (Superficial spreading, Nodular, lentigo, acral lentiginous, subungual, and amelanotic melanoma). A larger subset of 48,000 images was also generated specifically for CNN pre-training to avoid overfitting.
Stratified 10-fold cross-validation was used to ensure each class is represented with the same proportions as in the entire dataset during training and testing splits.
Ensemble diversity was applied to datasets to achieve better accuracy and avoid overlapping features during clustering.

conclusions: The Ensemble CNN model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 98.67%, outperforming other tested models including Boosted SVM (98.37%), Adaboost (96.78%), and Random Forest (90.23%).
Ensemble learning methods effectively reduce misclassification errors and overfitting issues in melanoma classification compared to individual classifiers.
Transfer learning and ensembled transfer learning approaches were necessary to mitigate imbalanced classification issues inherent in the six melanoma classes.

Classifiers performed better for Superficial spreading and Nodular melanoma classes, while performance was lower for Subungual melanoma due to structural variations and differences in image properties.
The Boosted SVM classifier showed significantly improved Mathew’s correlation coefficient (MCC) compared to conventional SVM and Adaboost models.
The Ensemble CNN model exhibited overfitting during validation despite achieving high accuracy, indicating a need for further network improvements and data selection.
Boosted GMM and Random Forest models showed signs of underfitting data points in the analysis.
GAN-generated synthetic data was successfully utilized to address class imbalance and provide sufficient training data for deep learning models, which require larger datasets to avoid overfitting.
important_remarks: The study relies entirely on GAN-generated synthetic data rather than real clinical dermoscopy images, which raises significant concerns about external validity and clinical applicability.
The specific GAN architecture and generation process are not detailed, limiting reproducibility and transparency regarding the synthetic data quality.
The Ensemble CNN model exhibited overfitting during validation despite achieving high accuracy (98.67%), indicating potential instability in the deep learning approach.
Classifiers performed significantly worse for Amelanotic melanoma compared to other classes, highlighting a limitation in handling this specific and clinically challenging subtype.
Subungual melanoma showed lower accuracy due to structural variations, suggesting the model may struggle with anatomical diversity in real-world scenarios.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) to compare model performances, relying instead on descriptive metrics.
No external validation on independent, real-world datasets was performed, which is a critical gap for assessing generalizability in clinical settings.
The dataset size and class distribution are artificial (balanced via GAN), which does not reflect the class imbalance typically found in real-world melanoma datasets.
High execution time for ensemble classifiers is noted as a complexity concern for potential deployment in resource-constrained environments.
